cards.title: Scaling Strategies
cards.explanation: Vertical scaling (scale-up) adds more CPU/RAM to one machine — simple but limited by hardware ceiling and creates a single point of failure. Horizontal scaling (scale-out) adds more machines — limitless but requires stateless services (session state must be externalised to Redis or a DB). Sticky sessions (same user always hits the same server) break horizontal scaling's benefits. Read replicas offload read traffic from the primary DB. Sharding splits data across multiple DB nodes by a shard key — enables write scaling but complicates cross-shard queries.
cards.whyItMatters: Every system design interview starts with 'how would you scale this?' Knowing the tools — stateless services, read replicas, sharding, consistent hashing — and their trade-offs lets you give structured, credible answers.
cards.gotcha: Sharding by user ID distributes writes evenly but makes 'find all orders from last week' a fan-out query across all shards. Shard key selection is the most consequential sharding decision — choose based on your most common query patterns, not just write distribution.

cards.title: Caching Strategies
cards.explanation: Cache-aside (lazy loading): application checks cache first; on miss, reads from DB and populates cache. Simple and resilient — cache failure doesn't break the app. Write-through: every DB write also updates the cache; data is always fresh but writes are slower. Write-behind (write-back): write to cache first, flush to DB asynchronously — fast writes but risk of data loss on crash. Cache eviction policies: LRU (least recently used) evicts the item not accessed for the longest time; LFU (least frequently used) evicts the item accessed fewest times.
cards.whyItMatters: Caching is the single most impactful performance optimisation in most web applications. A cache hit ratio of 95% means only 5% of requests hit the DB — a 20× reduction in DB load. Understanding TTL and eviction prevents serving stale data indefinitely.
cards.gotcha: Cache stampede (thundering herd): when a cached item expires, all concurrent requests miss and simultaneously hit the DB. Fix: probabilistic early expiration (re-cache when TTL is close, with random probability) or a lock that allows only one request to refresh while others wait for the result.

cards.title: Message Queues & Async Processing
cards.explanation: Message queues decouple producers from consumers temporally — producers publish without waiting for consumers to process. Kafka is a distributed log: messages are persisted, ordered within a partition, replayable, and consumed by multiple independent consumer groups. RabbitMQ is a traditional message broker: flexible routing (topic, fanout, direct exchanges), messages are deleted after consumption. Choose Kafka for event sourcing, audit logs, and stream processing; RabbitMQ for task queues, fanout notifications, and complex routing.
cards.whyItMatters: Async processing via queues is what separates scalable architectures from brittle ones. A checkout flow that synchronously calls payment, inventory, email, and shipping is one slow service away from timing out. Queues absorb spikes, enable retry, and let each service scale independently.
cards.gotcha: Kafka's at-least-once delivery guarantee means duplicate messages are possible (producer retry on network failure, consumer crash after processing but before committing). Every Kafka consumer must be idempotent — processing the same message twice must be safe.

cards.title: URL Shortener
cards.explanation: Requirements: shorten a URL to a 6–8 character code, redirect on access, handle 100M URLs, 10:1 read/write ratio. Core design: generate a unique ID (auto-increment or Snowflake), encode it to base62 (a-z A-Z 0-9), store the mapping in a DB. For redirects, use 301 (permanent — browser caches, reduces server load) or 302 (temporary — every request hits the server, enabling analytics). A Redis cache in front of the DB serves the read-heavy redirect path.
cards.whyItMatters: URL shortener is the canonical system design warm-up. It covers ID generation, encoding, DB schema, caching, and redirect semantics — all in a bounded, understandable problem. The same building blocks appear in QR codes, referral tracking, and deep linking.
cards.gotcha: Using hash(url) as the code causes collisions. Two different URLs can hash to the same code — requiring a collision resolution strategy. Using a generated ID (auto-increment or Snowflake) avoids collisions entirely and is simpler.

cards.title: Rate Limiter
cards.explanation: Rate limiting controls how many requests a client can make in a window. Token bucket: a bucket holds up to N tokens, refills at R tokens/second; each request consumes one token; requests when empty are rejected (429). Sliding window log: store timestamps of recent requests; reject if count in last window exceeds limit — precise but memory-intensive. Fixed window: simple counter per minute, but allows 2× the limit at window boundaries. For distributed rate limiting across multiple instances, use Redis as the shared counter with a Lua script for atomic check-and-decrement.
cards.whyItMatters: Rate limiting protects services from intentional (DoS) and unintentional (bug in client) overload. It ensures fair resource distribution — one misbehaving client can't degrade the service for all others. Every public API should have it.
cards.gotcha: IP-based rate limiting is easily bypassed with a botnet or shared NAT (an entire office behind one IP gets throttled together). Rate limit by API key or authenticated user ID for better precision. For unauthenticated endpoints, combine IP + fingerprinting.

cards.title: Notification System
cards.explanation: A notification system delivers messages across channels (push, email, SMS) at scale. Core challenges: fan-out (one event notifying millions of users), delivery guarantees (at-least-once + idempotency to avoid duplicates), channel preference management, and priority (critical alerts must not be queued behind marketing emails). Architecture: API receives notification request → publishes to Kafka topic per channel → channel workers (Push Worker, Email Worker, SMS Worker) consume and deliver → delivery status stored for audit.
cards.whyItMatters: Notification systems appear in nearly every product at scale. The patterns here — Kafka fan-out, per-channel workers, idempotency keys, priority queues — are reusable across alert systems, order updates, marketing campaigns, and real-time messaging.
cards.gotcha: Fan-out on write (writing to each user's inbox at publish time) works well for low follower counts but doesn't scale for influencers with millions of followers — a single post write triggers millions of inserts. Use fan-out on read (store once, expand per viewer) for high-fan-out entities, with a hybrid for medium accounts.
